title: In the Blink of an Eye
excerpt: A master film editor's meditation on the art and craft of editing, exploring why cuts work and how editing mirrors the process of human thought.
keyIdeas: The cut is the fundamental unit of film art — where meaning is created through juxtaposition
Editing mirrors the way human consciousness processes reality through discrete perceptions
The blink of an eye is the model for a well-timed cut — it corresponds to a moment of emotional closure
Emotion is the most important criterion for a good cut, not continuity or technical correctness
There are six criteria for a good cut, ranked by importance from emotion to eye-trace
Digital editing has changed the process but not the fundamental principles
keyTakeaways: Always cut for emotional truth first — everything else is secondary
A good cut feels inevitable — the audience should not notice it
Watch the audience's eyes to know where they expect a cut
The rhythm of editing should correspond to the emotional rhythm of the scene
Three-dimensional continuity matters more than two-dimensional screen direction
whoShouldRead: Film editors and aspiring editors seeking a philosophical foundation
Directors who want to understand how editing shapes their work
Film students learning the principles of cinematic storytelling
Video creators and YouTubers who want professional editing principles
Anyone interested in how movies create meaning through juxtaposition
whoShouldSkip: Novices who need a basic how-to guide before a philosophical treatise
Readers who prefer technical instruction over craft philosophy
People who are not interested in film as an art form
